intent: Check whether the candidate understands MongoDB's document model, BSON documents, collections, and why schema design is query-driven.
primaryQuestion: How would you explain the difference between a MongoDB document, a collection, and a relational database row/table, and how does that affect schema design?

expectedSignals.label: Explains documents as JSON-like BSON records grouped in collections.

requirements.description: Explains a MongoDB document as a JSON-like BSON record with fields and nested values
Explains a collection as a grouping of related documents
Compares documents and collections to relational rows and tables without claiming they behave identically

expectedSignals.label: Understands that related data can be embedded in a single document when access patterns fit.

requirements.description: Mentions embedding related data in the same document for data usually read together
Connects embedding to read and write access patterns rather than using it by default
Mentions bounded size, update frequency, or document growth as constraints on embedding

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that MongoDB does not remove the need for schema design; the schema is shaped around reads, writes, and update frequency.

requirements.description: States that MongoDB still needs intentional schema design even without fixed table columns
Shapes the document model around common queries and read paths
Considers how often fields or nested arrays change when choosing document shape

expectedSignals.label: Can compare flexibility with the risk of inconsistent document shapes.

requirements.description: Mentions flexibility for optional fields, evolving attributes, or heterogeneous documents
Mentions risk from documents in the same collection having incompatible shapes
Mentions schema validation, application conventions, or migrations to control shape drift

scoringHints.description: Clearly compares document and relational modeling, then connects the answer to real read/write access patterns.
Understands documents and collections but gives limited detail about schema trade-offs.
Knows MongoDB stores documents but treats modeling as arbitrary.
Answer is mostly buzzwords and does not show practical modeling understanding.

followUps.question: For a blog post with comments, when would you embed comments and when would you store them in a separate collection?
followUps.purpose: Force concrete reasoning about access patterns and growth.

followUps.question: What can go wrong if document shapes vary too much across the same collection?
followUps.purpose: Check awareness of validation, query complexity, and operational consistency.

followUps.question: Which reads and writes would drive your document shape for that blog model?
followUps.purpose: Tie schema design to concrete access patterns.

en.title: MongoDB document model basics
en.displayQuestion: How would you explain the difference between a MongoDB document, a collection, and a relational database row/table, and how does that affect schema design?
en.displayIntent: Check practical understanding of MongoDB documents, collections, and query-driven schema design.
guidance: Use one concrete domain example such as posts, orders, or users.
Connect flexible documents to both productivity and data consistency risks.
commonMistakes: Saying MongoDB has no schema design work.
Explaining only terminology without access patterns.

vi.title: Nền tảng document model MongoDB
vi.displayQuestion: Bạn giải thích sự khác nhau giữa document, collection trong MongoDB và row/table trong relational database như thế nào, và điều đó ảnh hưởng tới schema design ra sao?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra hiểu biết thực tế về document, collection và schema design theo access pattern trong MongoDB.
guidance: Dùng một ví dụ cụ thể như bài viết, đơn hàng hoặc người dùng.
Liên hệ document linh hoạt với cả tốc độ phát triển lẫn rủi ro nhất quán dữ liệu.
commonMistakes: Nói MongoDB không cần thiết kế schema.
Chỉ giải thích thuật ngữ mà không nói tới access pattern.

ja.title: MongoDB document model の基礎
ja.displayQuestion: MongoDB の document と collection、relational database の row/table の違いをどう説明し、それが schema design にどう影響しますか。
ja.displayIntent: MongoDB の document、collection、access pattern に基づく schema design の理解を確認する。
guidance: post、order、user など具体例を使う。
柔軟な document と data consistency のリスクを結びつける。
commonMistakes: MongoDB では schema design が不要だと言う。
用語だけを説明して access pattern に触れない。

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: Data Modeling Introduction
sourceReferences.note: Used for document modeling and access-pattern-driven design concepts.

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: BSON Types
sourceReferences.note: Used for MongoDB document storage terminology.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate can reason about basic MongoDB CRUD filters, projections, sorting, and update operators.
primaryQuestion: How would you query active users by email domain, return only needed fields, sort by creation time, and update one user's lastLoginAt in MongoDB?

expectedSignals.label: Builds a filter using fields and operators instead of scanning in application code.

requirements.description: Builds a MongoDB filter for active users using fields such as status or active
Filters by email domain through an appropriate field, derived domain, or anchored pattern rather than scanning every user in application code
Uses MongoDB query operators or field predicates intentionally instead of fetching all documents first

expectedSignals.label: Uses projection to avoid returning unnecessary fields.

requirements.description: Uses projection to return only fields needed by the response
Avoids returning sensitive fields such as password hashes, tokens, or internal metadata
Connects projection to smaller payloads, privacy, or lower application processing cost

expectedSignals.label: Mentions sort and limit when result ordering or size matters.

requirements.description: Sorts by creation time in the required direction for deterministic result order
Uses limit or pagination when the result set can be large
Mentions that sorting should be supported by an index when the result set is large

expectedSignals.label: Uses an update operator such as $set instead of replacing the whole document unintentionally.

requirements.description: Uses $set to update lastLoginAt without rewriting unrelated fields
Targets the intended user with updateOne or an equivalent precise filter
Explains the risk of replaceOne or full-document replacement when only one field should change

scoringHints.description: Describes filter, projection, sort, limit, and operator-based update with a realistic example.
Covers query and update basics but misses one detail such as projection or limit.
Can describe CRUD at a high level but lacks practical operator usage.
No clear MongoDB query or update model is demonstrated.

followUps.question: What fields would you exclude from the response, and why?
What is the risk of calling replaceOne when you only meant to update lastLoginAt?
What would the filter, projection, sort, and update operator look like for this user query?
followUps.purpose: Force a concrete MongoDB CRUD query shape.

en.title: MongoDB CRUD query shape
en.displayQuestion: How would you query active users by email domain, return only needed fields, sort by creation time, and update one user's lastLoginAt in MongoDB?
en.displayIntent: Check practical CRUD, filtering, projection, sorting, and update operator basics.
guidance: Ask for query shape rather than exact driver syntax.
Listen for projection and update semantics.
commonMistakes: Filtering large result sets in application memory.
Replacing a whole document when only one field should change.

vi.title: Dạng query CRUD trong MongoDB
vi.displayQuestion: Bạn sẽ query user đang active theo email domain, chỉ trả về các field cần thiết, sort theo thời điểm tạo và update lastLoginAt của một user trong MongoDB như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra nền tảng CRUD, filter, projection, sort và update operator trong MongoDB.
guidance: Tập trung vào shape của query, không bắt buộc đúng cú pháp driver từng ngôn ngữ.
Nghe xem ứng viên có nói tới projection và ngữ nghĩa update không.
commonMistakes: Lấy nhiều dữ liệu rồi filter trong memory của application.
Replace toàn bộ document khi chỉ cần đổi một field.

ja.title: MongoDB CRUD query の形
ja.displayQuestion: active user を email domain で検索し、必要な field だけ返し、creation time で sort し、1 user の lastLoginAt を更新するにはどうしますか。
ja.displayIntent: CRUD、filter、projection、sort、update operator の基礎を確認する。
guidance: driver の完全な構文より query の形を確認する。
projection と update semantics に注目する。
commonMistakes: 大量データを application memory で filter する。
1 field だけ変えるつもりで document 全体を replace する。

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: Query Documents
sourceReferences.note: Used for filters and query operators.

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: Update Documents
sourceReferences.note: Used for update operator expectations.

intent: Assess basic operational debugging when an application cannot connect to MongoDB.
primaryQuestion: A Node.js service suddenly cannot connect to MongoDB after deployment. What would you check before changing application code?

expectedSignals.label: Checks connection string, credentials, database name, and environment variables.

requirements.description: Checks the MongoDB connection string, host, scheme, options, and database name
Checks username, password, auth source, and credential rotation without exposing secrets
Compares deployed environment variables or secret references with the expected deployment configuration

expectedSignals.label: Verifies network path, DNS, firewall, allowlist, and TLS settings.

requirements.description: Checks DNS or SRV record resolution from the deployed environment
Checks firewall rules, IP allowlists, VPC peering, or network reachability
Checks TLS, certificate, or driver connection options when the error points to handshake problems

expectedSignals.label: Looks at driver error messages and database/server logs.

requirements.description: Uses the exact driver error message, error code, or timeout type to guide debugging
Checks MongoDB server, Atlas, or deployment logs for authentication, network, or topology errors
Correlates logs and errors with the deployment time and changed configuration

expectedSignals.label: Considers deployment differences such as secrets, replica set name, or SRV record resolution.

requirements.description: Compares working and failing environments to identify deployment-specific differences
Checks replica set name, direct connection, or topology options when relevant
Checks whether secrets were mounted, injected, or decoded correctly in the new deployment

scoringHints.description: Uses an evidence-based checklist across config, network, credentials, TLS, and logs before code changes.
Covers config and credentials but misses one operational layer such as network or TLS.
Mentions generic debugging but lacks MongoDB connection specifics.
No clear investigation sequence.

followUps.question: What exact information from the driver error would change your next step?
How would you debug credentials without exposing secrets in logs?

en.title: Debug MongoDB connection failure
en.displayQuestion: A Node.js service suddenly cannot connect to MongoDB after deployment. What would you check before changing application code?
en.displayIntent: Evaluate basic MongoDB connection troubleshooting in a deployed service.
guidance: Start from the actual error message.
Cover config, network, credentials, and deployment differences.
commonMistakes: Changing code before checking environment configuration.
Logging secrets while debugging.

vi.title: Debug lỗi kết nối MongoDB
vi.displayQuestion: Một service Node.js sau khi deploy đột nhiên không kết nối được MongoDB. Trước khi sửa code application, bạn sẽ kiểm tra những gì?
vi.displayIntent: Đánh giá khả năng troubleshoot kết nối MongoDB cơ bản trong môi trường đã deploy.
guidance: Bắt đầu từ error message thật.
Bao phủ config, network, credentials và khác biệt giữa môi trường.
commonMistakes: Sửa code trước khi kiểm tra environment configuration.
Log secret trong lúc debug.

ja.title: MongoDB 接続失敗の debug
ja.displayQuestion: deploy 後に Node.js service が MongoDB に接続できなくなりました。application code を変える前に何を確認しますか。
ja.displayIntent: deployed service での MongoDB 接続 troubleshooting を評価する。
guidance: 実際の error message から始める。
config、network、credentials、deployment 差分を確認する。
commonMistakes: environment configuration を確認せず code を変更する。
debug 中に secret を log に出す。

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: Connection String URI Format
sourceReferences.note: Used for connection string, authentication, and replica set options.

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Node.js Driver Docs: Connect to MongoDB
sourceReferences.note: Used for application connection troubleshooting context.

intent: Check whether the candidate understands what indexes do, why they speed reads, and what trade-offs they introduce.
primaryQuestion: What is a MongoDB index, how can it help a query, and what costs should you expect when adding indexes?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that indexes let MongoDB find matching documents without scanning the whole collection.

requirements.description: Explains that an index lets MongoDB locate matching documents through indexed keys
Contrasts indexed access with scanning the whole collection
Connects index value to selective filters or ordered access patterns

expectedSignals.label: Mentions index keys should match common filters, sorts, or uniqueness requirements.

requirements.description: Chooses index keys based on common query filter fields
Considers sort fields and direction when index ordering matters
Mentions unique indexes when the domain requires uniqueness such as users.email

expectedSignals.label: Understands indexes consume storage and add write maintenance overhead.

requirements.description: Mentions additional storage consumed by indexes
Mentions insert, update, or delete overhead from maintaining indexes
Mentions operational maintenance, build time, or memory/cache impact from indexes

expectedSignals.label: Can distinguish a useful index from adding indexes to every field.

requirements.description: Rejects adding indexes to every field by default
Uses query workload, explain output, or slow query data to justify an index
Balances read improvement against write overhead and storage cost

scoringHints.description: Explains read benefit, write/storage cost, and query-shape alignment with a concrete example.
Understands basic index purpose but gives limited trade-off detail.
Knows indexes are for performance but cannot explain how or when.
Answer does not demonstrate MongoDB index understanding.

followUps.question: Why might too many indexes slow down a write-heavy collection?
What query pattern would justify an index on users.email?
followUps.purpose: Connect index choice to a real access pattern.

followUps.question: How would you explain the difference between an index scan and a collection scan?
followUps.purpose: Check basic index mechanics in concrete terms.

en.title: MongoDB index fundamentals
en.displayQuestion: What is a MongoDB index, how can it help a query, and what costs should you expect when adding indexes?
en.displayIntent: Check basic index reasoning and read/write trade-offs.
guidance: Ask for a simple query example.
Listen for both query speed and write/storage cost.
commonMistakes: Treating indexes as free performance.
Indexing every field without access-pattern evidence.

vi.title: Nền tảng index MongoDB
vi.displayQuestion: Index trong MongoDB là gì, nó giúp query như thế nào, và khi thêm index bạn cần chấp nhận những chi phí nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra tư duy index cơ bản và trade-off giữa read/write.
guidance: Yêu cầu một ví dụ query đơn giản.
Lắng nghe cả lợi ích tốc độ query lẫn chi phí write/storage.
commonMistakes: Xem index như hiệu năng miễn phí.
Index mọi field mà không có bằng chứng access pattern.

ja.title: MongoDB index の基礎
ja.displayQuestion: MongoDB index とは何で、query をどう助け、index 追加時にどんな cost を考えますか。
ja.displayIntent: 基本的な index reasoning と read/write trade-off を確認する。
guidance: 簡単な query example を使う。
query speed と write/storage cost の両方を聞く。
commonMistakes: index を無料の performance 改善と考える。
access pattern なしに全 field に index を作る。

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: Indexes
sourceReferences.note: Used for index purpose and performance trade-offs.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate can choose between embedding and referencing for a simple product model.
primaryQuestion: You are modeling orders and order items in MongoDB. Would you embed the items inside the order document or reference them from another collection?

expectedSignals.label: Starts from how orders and items are read and updated.

requirements.description: Identifies whether order items are usually read together with the order
Identifies whether line items are updated independently after order creation
Considers whether queries need order-level data, item-level searches, or both

expectedSignals.label: Chooses embedding when items are usually retrieved with the order and bounded in size.

requirements.description: Chooses embedding when the order and its items are commonly retrieved as one aggregate
States that embedded items should be bounded in count or document size
Mentions that embedding can make order-level updates atomic within one document

expectedSignals.label: Considers references if items are large, independently updated, or shared across many documents.

requirements.description: Considers references when the item list can become large or unbounded
Considers references when items need independent updates or lifecycle
Considers references for entities shared across many documents

expectedSignals.label: Mentions document growth and maximum document size as constraints.

requirements.description: Mentions document growth as line items are added or modified
Mentions MongoDB's maximum document size constraint at a practical level
Mentions performance or update concerns with very large embedded arrays

scoringHints.description: Makes a contextual recommendation using read locality, update patterns, boundedness, and document growth constraints.
Chooses a reasonable design but misses one important constraint.
Gives a rule of thumb without asking about access patterns.
Cannot justify embedding versus referencing.

followUps.question: What changes if an order can have tens of thousands of line items?
followUps.purpose: Check bounded array and document growth reasoning.

followUps.question: What query becomes easier with embedding, and what update can become harder?

en.title: Embed or reference order items
en.displayQuestion: You are modeling orders and order items in MongoDB. Would you embed the items inside the order document or reference them from another collection?
en.displayIntent: Assess basic schema design trade-offs for embedding versus references.
guidance: Ask the candidate to state assumptions about read and update patterns.
Look for boundedness and document growth concerns.
commonMistakes: Treating embedding as always correct.
Ignoring very large or independently updated child records.

vi.title: Embed hay reference order items
vi.displayQuestion: Bạn đang modeling orders và order items trong MongoDB. Bạn sẽ embed items vào order document hay reference từ collection khác?
vi.displayIntent: Đánh giá trade-off schema design cơ bản giữa embedding và reference.
guidance: Yêu cầu ứng viên nêu giả định về read và update pattern.
Tìm tín hiệu về boundedness và document growth.
commonMistakes: Xem embedding luôn là đúng.
Bỏ qua child record rất lớn hoặc được update độc lập.

ja.title: order item を embed するか reference するか
ja.displayQuestion: MongoDB で orders と order items を model します。items を order document に embed しますか、それとも別 collection から reference しますか。
ja.displayIntent: embedding と reference の基本的な schema design trade-off を評価する。
guidance: read/update pattern の仮定を話してもらう。
boundedness と document growth に注意する。
commonMistakes: embedding が常に正しいと考える。
非常に大きい、または独立更新される child record を無視する。

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: Embedded Data Models
sourceReferences.note: Used for embedding guidance.

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: References
sourceReferences.note: Used for reference model trade-offs.

intent: Check whether the candidate understands the aggregation pipeline as ordered data transformation stages.
primaryQuestion: What is the MongoDB aggregation pipeline, and how would you use $match, $group, and $sort to calculate daily order revenue?

expectedSignals.label: Explains aggregation as a sequence of stages where documents flow through transformations.

requirements.description: Explains that an aggregation pipeline processes documents through ordered stages
Mentions that each stage can filter, reshape, group, or sort the documents flowing through it
Connects aggregation to server-side reporting or transformation rather than application-side loops

expectedSignals.label: Uses $match early to filter relevant orders.

requirements.description: Uses $match to filter only the orders relevant to the report
Places $match before $group when possible to reduce documents entering expensive stages
Mentions that early $match can use indexes when it appears before transformations that prevent index use

expectedSignals.label: Groups by a date bucket and sums order amounts.

requirements.description: Groups orders by a daily date bucket or equivalent day key
Uses $sum or equivalent aggregation to calculate total order revenue
Explains that $group changes the result shape to one document per group

expectedSignals.label: Sorts results and considers date/timezone boundaries at a basic level.

requirements.description: Sorts the daily revenue output by date or another intentional ordering
Mentions timezone or business-day boundaries when grouping revenue by day
Considers inclusive/exclusive date range boundaries for the report

scoringHints.description: Builds a sensible pipeline and explains stage order, grouping key, sum, and date boundary assumptions.
Understands pipeline stages but misses timezone or stage-order nuance.
Names stages but cannot connect them into a working pipeline.
No clear aggregation pipeline understanding.

followUps.question: Why would you usually put $match before $group?
followUps.purpose: Check stage ordering and performance intuition.

followUps.question: How would timezone affect a daily revenue report?
What documents or fields would the pipeline output after $group for daily revenue?
followUps.purpose: Check whether the candidate understands aggregation result shape.

en.title: Aggregation pipeline basics
en.displayQuestion: What is the MongoDB aggregation pipeline, and how would you use $match, $group, and $sort to calculate daily order revenue?
en.displayIntent: Check basic aggregation pipeline understanding.
guidance: Focus on stage order and data shape after each stage.
Ask for a daily revenue example.
commonMistakes: Grouping before filtering without reason.
Ignoring date boundaries in reporting.

vi.title: Nền tảng aggregation pipeline
vi.displayQuestion: Aggregation pipeline trong MongoDB là gì, và bạn sẽ dùng $match, $group, $sort như thế nào để tính doanh thu order theo ngày?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra hiểu biết cơ bản về aggregation pipeline.
guidance: Tập trung vào thứ tự stage và shape dữ liệu sau mỗi stage.
Yêu cầu ví dụ doanh thu theo ngày.
commonMistakes: Group trước khi filter mà không có lý do.
Bỏ qua boundary ngày khi làm reporting.

ja.title: aggregation pipeline の基礎
ja.displayQuestion: MongoDB aggregation pipeline とは何で、$match、$group、$sort を使って daily order revenue をどう計算しますか。
ja.displayIntent: aggregation pipeline の基本理解を確認する。
guidance: stage order と各 stage 後の data shape に注目する。
daily revenue example を使う。
commonMistakes: 理由なく filter 前に group する。
reporting で日付 boundary を無視する。

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: Aggregation Pipeline
sourceReferences.note: Used for pipeline stage model.

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: Aggregation Stages
sourceReferences.note: Used for $match, $group, and $sort stage expectations.

intent: Assess mid-level understanding of compound index ordering, query shape, sort support, and covered queries.
primaryQuestion: For a collection of events queried by tenantId, status, createdAt range, and sorted by createdAt desc, how would you design a compound index and verify it is being used?

expectedSignals.label: Chooses an index order based on equality predicates, range predicate, and sort requirement.

requirements.description: Considers leading equality fields such as tenantId and possibly status before range fields
Accounts for createdAt range filtering and createdAt descending sort in the compound index
Explains how field order affects filtering, sort support, and index scan efficiency

expectedSignals.label: Explains why tenantId is usually a leading key in multi-tenant queries.

requirements.description: Uses tenantId early when most queries are tenant-scoped
Explains tenantId selectivity or isolation as a reason to lead the index
Explains that omitting tenantId can scan across many tenants or reduce isolation of workload

expectedSignals.label: Uses explain plans to inspect IXSCAN, keys examined, documents examined, and sort behavior.

requirements.description: Uses explain to confirm an IXSCAN or intended index access path
Compares keys examined and documents examined to expected result size
Checks whether the plan avoids an expensive blocking sort or in-memory sort

expectedSignals.label: Mentions covered queries or projection when relevant.

requirements.description: Mentions covered queries when all required fields can be satisfied from the index
Uses projection to limit returned fields when trying to achieve coverage
Balances covered-query benefit against larger index size or write overhead

scoringHints.description: Proposes a defensible compound index, explains key order trade-offs, and validates with explain metrics.
Understands compound indexes but is light on explain analysis or sort coverage.
Knows indexes help but cannot reason about compound key order.
No practical index design or verification approach.

followUps.question: What changes if status has very low cardinality but tenantId is highly selective?
followUps.purpose: Check selectivity and key order reasoning.

followUps.question: Which explain output fields would convince you the index improved the query?
What would make this query covered by the compound index?

en.title: Compound index for event queries
en.displayQuestion: For a collection of events queried by tenantId, status, createdAt range, and sorted by createdAt desc, how would you design a compound index and verify it is being used?
en.displayIntent: Evaluate compound index design and query-plan verification.
guidance: Ask the candidate to justify index key order.
Expect explain-plan evidence, not just index creation.
commonMistakes: Relying only on many single-field indexes.
Ignoring sort and range interactions.

vi.title: Compound index cho event query
vi.displayQuestion: Với collection events được query theo tenantId, status, range createdAt và sort createdAt desc, bạn thiết kế compound index thế nào và xác minh nó được dùng ra sao?
vi.displayIntent: Đánh giá thiết kế compound index và kiểm chứng query plan.
guidance: Yêu cầu ứng viên giải thích thứ tự field trong index.
Cần bằng chứng từ explain plan, không chỉ nói tạo index.
commonMistakes: Chỉ dựa vào nhiều single-field index.
Bỏ qua tương tác giữa sort và range.

ja.title: event query 向け compound index
ja.displayQuestion: tenantId、status、createdAt range で query し createdAt desc で sort する events collection に、どんな compound index を作り、使用をどう確認しますか。
ja.displayIntent: compound index design と query plan verification を評価する。
guidance: index key order の理由を説明してもらう。
index 作成だけでなく explain plan の証拠を期待する。
commonMistakes: 多数の single-field index だけに頼る。
sort と range の相互作用を無視する。

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: Compound Indexes
sourceReferences.note: Used for compound index and prefix behavior.

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: Explain Results
sourceReferences.note: Used for query plan verification signals.

intent: Evaluate systematic diagnosis of a slow MongoDB query in production.
primaryQuestion: A MongoDB endpoint changed from 80 ms to 3 seconds after data growth. How would you diagnose whether the issue is index design, query shape, or data distribution?

expectedSignals.label: Captures the exact query, parameters, projection, sort, and collection size.

requirements.description: Captures the exact MongoDB query filter and parameter values used by the slow endpoint
Captures projection, sort, limit, and pagination behavior for the query
Captures collection size, relevant subset size, and data growth since the fast baseline

expectedSignals.label: Uses explain execution stats or profiler/slow query logs to compare keys examined, docs examined, and execution time.

requirements.description: Uses explain execution stats to inspect actual query work
Uses profiler output or slow query logs when diagnosing production behavior
Compares keys examined, docs examined, returned docs, and execution time

expectedSignals.label: Checks whether the query performs COLLSCAN, blocking sort, or reads too many documents.

requirements.description: Checks whether the plan performs a COLLSCAN instead of using an index
Checks whether sorting is unsupported by the index and requires a blocking sort
Checks whether documents examined is much larger than documents returned

expectedSignals.label: Considers data skew, tenant distribution, and changes in cardinality after growth.

requirements.description: Considers that one tenant or segment may have much more data than others
Considers changes in field cardinality or selectivity after data growth
Tests with representative parameter values instead of only average or local cases

scoringHints.description: Runs a measured investigation from query capture to plan analysis, data distribution, fix, and validation.
Good explain-based debugging but limited discussion of data growth or skew.
Mentions indexes but lacks a systematic production diagnosis.
No concrete evidence-gathering path.

followUps.question: Which before-and-after metrics would prove your fix worked?
Why can the same query shape be fast for one tenant and slow for another?

en.title: Diagnose slow MongoDB query
en.displayQuestion: A MongoDB endpoint changed from 80 ms to 3 seconds after data growth. How would you diagnose whether the issue is index design, query shape, or data distribution?
en.displayIntent: Evaluate production slow-query debugging.
guidance: Look for evidence from explain, logs, or profiler.
Ask how the candidate validates the fix after deployment.
commonMistakes: Creating indexes without reading the query plan.
Testing only with small local data.

vi.title: Diagnose query MongoDB chậm
vi.displayQuestion: Một endpoint MongoDB tăng từ 80 ms lên 3 giây sau khi dữ liệu lớn lên. Bạn diagnose thế nào để biết vấn đề nằm ở index design, query shape hay data distribution?
vi.displayIntent: Đánh giá khả năng debug slow query trong production.
guidance: Tìm bằng chứng từ explain, log hoặc profiler.
Hỏi cách ứng viên validate fix sau khi deploy.
commonMistakes: Tạo index mà không đọc query plan.
Chỉ test với dữ liệu local nhỏ.

ja.title: 遅い MongoDB query の診断
ja.displayQuestion: data growth 後、MongoDB endpoint が 80 ms から 3 秒になりました。index design、query shape、data distribution のどこが原因かどう診断しますか。
ja.displayIntent: production slow query debugging を評価する。
guidance: explain、log、profiler から証拠を探す。
deploy 後に fix をどう検証するか聞く。
commonMistakes: query plan を読まずに index を作る。
小さい local data だけで test する。

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: Analyze Query Performance
sourceReferences.note: Used for explain-based slow query diagnosis.

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: Database Profiler
sourceReferences.note: Used for slow query observation techniques.

intent: Assess nuanced schema modeling for one-to-many and many-to-many relationships beyond basic embedding advice.
primaryQuestion: For a product catalog with products, categories, variants, inventory, and reviews, what would you embed, what would you reference, and why?

expectedSignals.label: Separates read-heavy product display data from frequently changing inventory or reviews.

requirements.description: Keeps read-heavy product display fields near the product document when they are commonly rendered together
Treats inventory as frequently changing data that may need separate documents or careful update boundaries
Treats reviews as potentially growing and independently paginated rather than blindly embedding all reviews

expectedSignals.label: Considers bounded versus unbounded arrays.

requirements.description: Identifies arrays that are naturally small or bounded, such as a limited variant list
Identifies arrays that can grow without bound, such as reviews or events
Uses references or separate collections when unbounded growth would harm document size or update performance

expectedSignals.label: Mentions denormalization for read performance and the need to keep duplicated data consistent.

requirements.description: Accepts duplicating selected product, category, or summary fields to speed common reads
Explains how duplicated fields are updated, refreshed, or tolerated as eventually consistent
Balances faster product reads against update fan-out and stale data risk

expectedSignals.label: Explains how category and search/filter access patterns affect the model.

requirements.description: Considers category browsing or many-to-many category relationships when choosing references or duplicated category data
Considers filters by variant, price, availability, category, or attributes in the document/index design
Connects catalog access patterns to indexes or search-oriented projections

scoringHints.description: Balances embedded snapshots, references, denormalization, growth limits, and update consistency with product-specific access patterns.
Reasonable modeling choices but some edge cases such as reviews or inventory are underdeveloped.
Applies a simple rule without considering query and update behavior.
No clear model or trade-off explanation.

followUps.question: What duplicated data would you accept, and how would you keep it consistent enough?
How would your model change if product reviews are paginated and can grow without bound?

en.title: Catalog schema trade-offs
en.displayQuestion: For a product catalog with products, categories, variants, inventory, and reviews, what would you embed, what would you reference, and why?
en.displayIntent: Assess nuanced MongoDB schema modeling for mixed access patterns.
guidance: Ask for read and update patterns before accepting the design.
Expect discussion of denormalization and unbounded growth.
commonMistakes: Embedding unbounded reviews in one product document.
Ignoring inventory update frequency.

vi.title: Trade-off schema catalog
vi.displayQuestion: Với product catalog gồm products, categories, variants, inventory và reviews, bạn sẽ embed phần nào, reference phần nào, và vì sao?
vi.displayIntent: Đánh giá khả năng modeling MongoDB có nhiều access pattern khác nhau.
guidance: Yêu cầu read và update pattern trước khi chấp nhận thiết kế.
Kỳ vọng thảo luận về denormalization và tăng trưởng không giới hạn.
commonMistakes: Embed reviews không giới hạn trong một product document.
Bỏ qua tần suất update inventory.

ja.title: catalog schema の trade-off
ja.displayQuestion: products、categories、variants、inventory、reviews を持つ product catalog で、何を embed し何を reference し、その理由は何ですか。
ja.displayIntent: 複数 access pattern を持つ MongoDB schema modeling を評価する。
guidance: design を評価する前に read/update pattern を聞く。
denormalization と unbounded growth の説明を期待する。
commonMistakes: unbounded reviews を 1 product document に embed する。
inventory update frequency を無視する。

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: Data Model Design
sourceReferences.note: Used for access pattern and schema design process.

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: Embedding vs References
sourceReferences.note: Used for relationship modeling trade-offs.

intent: Evaluate aggregation pipeline depth including joins, grouping, unwinding arrays, and performance considerations.
primaryQuestion: How would you build an aggregation that joins orders to customers, unwinds line items, groups revenue by customer segment, and keeps the pipeline performant?

expectedSignals.label: Uses $lookup for collection joins and understands the join field requirements.

requirements.description: Uses $lookup to join orders to customers or customer segment data
Identifies localField and foreignField or pipeline join conditions correctly
Considers join cardinality and index support on the foreign collection

expectedSignals.label: Uses $unwind when aggregating array elements such as line items.

requirements.description: Uses $unwind on lineItems or equivalent array before summing item-level revenue
Explains that $unwind can multiply documents and increase downstream work
Considers preserveNullAndEmptyArrays or filtering behavior when arrays are empty or missing

expectedSignals.label: Groups by customer segment and sums revenue correctly.

requirements.description: Groups by customer segment or an equivalent joined customer attribute
Sums line item price times quantity or the correct order amount basis
Avoids double-counting revenue after $lookup or $unwind changes cardinality

expectedSignals.label: Pushes filtering and projection early and considers indexes on join/filter fields.

requirements.description: Places $match early to reduce orders entering lookup, unwind, and group stages
Uses $project to keep only fields needed by later stages when useful
Considers indexes on filter fields and join fields used by the aggregation

scoringHints.description: Describes a correct pipeline and performance controls around $match, $project, $lookup, $unwind, and $group.
Builds a plausible pipeline but performance reasoning is incomplete.
Knows aggregation terms but cannot reason through joins and arrays.
No usable pipeline design.

followUps.question: What happens to document count after $unwind, and why does that matter?
How would you inspect whether $lookup is the expensive stage?
followUps.purpose: Assess explain and pipeline optimization knowledge.

followUps.question: After joining and unwinding, what exactly would you group by and sum?

en.title: Aggregation with lookup and group
en.displayQuestion: How would you build an aggregation that joins orders to customers, unwinds line items, groups revenue by customer segment, and keeps the pipeline performant?
en.displayIntent: Evaluate practical aggregation design with joins, arrays, grouping, and performance.
guidance: Ask the candidate to describe stage order.
Listen for index and cardinality considerations.
commonMistakes: Using application-side N+1 queries for a report.
Ignoring how $unwind multiplies rows.

vi.title: Aggregation với lookup và group
vi.displayQuestion: Bạn sẽ xây aggregation join orders với customers, unwind line items, group revenue theo customer segment và giữ pipeline performant như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Đánh giá thiết kế aggregation thực tế với join, array, grouping và performance.
guidance: Yêu cầu ứng viên mô tả thứ tự stage.
Lắng nghe cân nhắc về index và cardinality.
commonMistakes: Dùng N+1 query ở application cho báo cáo phù hợp với aggregation.
Bỏ qua việc $unwind làm nhân số document.

ja.title: lookup と group を使う aggregation
ja.displayQuestion: orders と customers を join し、line items を unwind し、customer segment ごとに revenue を group し、pipeline を performant に保つにはどうしますか。
ja.displayIntent: join、array、grouping、performance を含む aggregation design を評価する。
guidance: stage order を説明してもらう。
index と cardinality の考慮を確認する。
commonMistakes: report に application-side N+1 queries を使う。
$unwind が document 数を増やすことを無視する。

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: $lookup
sourceReferences.note: Used for aggregation join behavior.

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: $unwind
sourceReferences.note: Used for array deconstruction behavior.

intent: Assess understanding of single-document atomicity, multi-document transactions, and when transactions are appropriate.
primaryQuestion: MongoDB writes are atomic at what level, and when would you use a multi-document transaction instead of modeling data to update one document?

expectedSignals.label: Knows single-document writes are atomic.

requirements.description: States that MongoDB write operations are atomic at the single-document level
Explains that related embedded fields can be updated atomically within one document
Avoids claiming that unrelated writes across documents or collections are automatically atomic

expectedSignals.label: Uses multi-document transactions for invariants across multiple documents or collections.

requirements.description: Names an invariant that spans multiple documents or collections
Uses a multi-document transaction when that invariant must commit or roll back together
Keeps transaction scope focused on the required consistency boundary

expectedSignals.label: Considers embedding or denormalization to keep common atomic updates within one document when appropriate.

requirements.description: Considers changing the document model before adding transactions to every workflow
Embeds or denormalizes data when common atomic updates naturally belong in one aggregate
Balances single-document atomicity against document growth, duplication, and update complexity

expectedSignals.label: Mentions retry/error handling and performance/latency overhead for transactions.

requirements.description: Mentions retrying transient transaction errors safely
Mentions additional latency, coordination, or resource overhead from transactions
Mentions idempotent application behavior when retrying transaction work

scoringHints.description: Explains atomicity boundaries, transaction use cases, schema alternatives, and operational costs.
Understands transactions but misses retry or modeling trade-offs.
Has a partial idea of transactions but not MongoDB atomicity boundaries.
Cannot reason about consistency guarantees.

followUps.question: What would make you redesign the document model instead of adding a transaction?
What retry behavior should application code have around transient transaction errors?
What exactly is atomic when you update one MongoDB document?
followUps.purpose: Force clarity on the atomicity boundary.

en.title: MongoDB atomicity and transactions
en.displayQuestion: MongoDB writes are atomic at what level, and when would you use a multi-document transaction instead of modeling data to update one document?
en.displayIntent: Check consistency reasoning around single-document atomicity and multi-document transactions.
guidance: Ask for a business invariant example.
Look for transaction costs and retry handling.
commonMistakes: Assuming all cross-collection writes are automatically atomic.
Using transactions as a substitute for all schema design.

vi.title: Atomicity và transaction MongoDB
vi.displayQuestion: Write trong MongoDB atomic ở cấp nào, và khi nào bạn dùng multi-document transaction thay vì modeling dữ liệu để update trong một document?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra tư duy consistency quanh single-document atomicity và multi-document transaction.
guidance: Yêu cầu ví dụ về business invariant.
Tìm tín hiệu về chi phí transaction và retry handling.
commonMistakes: Tưởng mọi write qua nhiều collection tự động atomic.
Dùng transaction thay cho mọi quyết định schema design.

ja.title: MongoDB atomicity と transaction
ja.displayQuestion: MongoDB write はどの level で atomic ですか。1 document update に model する代わりに multi-document transaction を使うのはいつですか。
ja.displayIntent: single-document atomicity と multi-document transaction の consistency reasoning を確認する。
guidance: business invariant の例を聞く。
transaction cost と retry handling を確認する。
commonMistakes: 複数 collection への write が自動的に atomic だと思う。
schema design の代わりにすべて transaction を使う。

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: Transactions
sourceReferences.note: Used for multi-document transaction semantics.

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: Atomicity and Transactions
sourceReferences.note: Used for single-document atomicity guidance.

intent: Evaluate debugging of perceived missing writes or stale reads using read/write concern and replication lag concepts.
primaryQuestion: Users report that a saved change sometimes disappears for a few seconds after refresh. How would you investigate read preference, read concern, write concern, and replication lag?

expectedSignals.label: Checks whether writes are acknowledged with an appropriate write concern.

requirements.description: Checks the configured write concern for the save operation
Distinguishes acknowledged writes from majority-durable writes
Checks write result, exceptions, timeouts, or write concern errors before assuming data loss

expectedSignals.label: Verifies if reads may be served from secondaries through read preference.

requirements.description: Checks whether the application uses secondary, secondaryPreferred, nearest, or other non-primary read preference
Explains that secondary reads can lag behind primary writes
Checks routing through drivers, services, or read endpoints that may differ after refresh

expectedSignals.label: Considers replication lag and session/causal consistency for read-your-writes behavior.

requirements.description: Mentions replication lag as a cause of temporarily stale reads
Mentions sessions or causal consistency for read-your-writes expectations
Balances reading from primary for freshness against latency or load trade-offs

expectedSignals.label: Uses logs/metrics to compare primary writes and secondary read timing.

requirements.description: Correlates write acknowledgment time with the later read time and target node
Checks replication lag metrics or replica status during the incident window
Uses driver, application, or database logs to see which node handled reads and writes

scoringHints.description: Connects the symptom to read/write concerns, read preference, replication lag, and concrete observability checks.
Understands replica lag and write acknowledgment but misses one consistency control.
Knows replication exists but cannot reason about stale reads.
No reliable consistency debugging approach.

followUps.question: What read preference would make stale reads more likely?
What metric or log would show replication lag during the incident?
How would you tell whether the write was not acknowledged or the later read was stale?
followUps.purpose: Separate lost-write suspicion from stale-read diagnosis.

followUps.question: When would you force reads to primary, and what cost might that add?

en.title: Debug stale MongoDB reads
en.displayQuestion: Users report that a saved change sometimes disappears for a few seconds after refresh. How would you investigate read preference, read concern, write concern, and replication lag?
en.displayIntent: Evaluate consistency debugging for stale reads or delayed visibility.
guidance: Separate lost writes from stale reads.
Expect discussion of read path, write acknowledgment, and lag metrics.
commonMistakes: Calling it data loss before checking read routing.
Confusing read concern, write concern, and read preference.

vi.title: Debug stale read MongoDB
vi.displayQuestion: User báo rằng thay đổi đã lưu đôi khi biến mất vài giây sau khi refresh. Bạn điều tra read preference, read concern, write concern và replication lag như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Đánh giá debug consistency cho stale read hoặc visibility delay.
guidance: Tách mất write thật khỏi stale read.
Kỳ vọng thảo luận về read path, write acknowledgment và lag metric.
commonMistakes: Kết luận data loss trước khi kiểm tra read routing.
Nhầm read concern, write concern và read preference.

ja.title: MongoDB stale read の debug
ja.displayQuestion: 保存した変更が refresh 後に数秒だけ消えたように見えると user が報告しました。read preference、read concern、write concern、replication lag をどう調査しますか。
ja.displayIntent: stale read や delayed visibility の consistency debugging を評価する。
guidance: lost write と stale read を分ける。
read path、write acknowledgment、lag metrics を確認する。
commonMistakes: read routing を確認せず data loss と判断する。
read concern、write concern、read preference を混同する。

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: Write Concern
sourceReferences.note: Used for acknowledged write behavior.

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: Read Preference
sourceReferences.note: Used for primary and secondary read routing.

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: Read Concern
sourceReferences.note: Used for read isolation and consistency behavior.

intent: Assess ability to design retention and archiving behavior using TTL indexes and operational constraints.
primaryQuestion: You need to keep session documents for 30 days and audit events for 7 years. Would you use TTL indexes, an archive job, or another design?

expectedSignals.label: Uses TTL indexes for simple automatic expiration of time-based data such as sessions.

requirements.description: Uses a TTL index for session documents with a clear expiration timestamp
Explains that TTL fits simple time-based deletion with no complex retention workflow
Avoids using TTL as a universal retention solution for data with compliance or archive needs

expectedSignals.label: Recognizes audit data often has legal, reporting, and restore requirements that may need archiving instead of simple deletion.

requirements.description: Mentions legal, compliance, or audit retention requirements for seven-year data
Considers archive collections, cold storage, or external archival systems instead of simple deletion
Considers restore, reporting, and access requirements for historical audit events

expectedSignals.label: Mentions TTL deletion is asynchronous and should not be treated as exact scheduling.

requirements.description: Mentions TTL cleanup happens asynchronously in the background
Avoids assuming documents disappear exactly at the expiration second
Plans for some lag or monitoring around expiration cleanup

expectedSignals.label: Considers indexes, storage growth, backup/restore, and query patterns for retained data.

requirements.description: Considers indexes for expected historical audit queries
Plans storage growth and cost for seven years of audit data
Considers backup and restore behavior for both hot and archived retention data

scoringHints.description: Separates ephemeral and regulated data, chooses TTL or archive deliberately, and covers operational constraints.
Good TTL understanding but compliance or operational details are thin.
Knows TTL exists but cannot choose a retention strategy.
No practical retention design.

followUps.question: Why is TTL a good fit for sessions but risky as the only mechanism for audit records?
How would your design support querying last year's audit events?
Why should TTL cleanup not be treated as an exact scheduler?

en.title: TTL versus archive retention
en.displayQuestion: You need to keep session documents for 30 days and audit events for 7 years. Would you use TTL indexes, an archive job, or another design?
en.displayIntent: Evaluate MongoDB retention design and TTL trade-offs.
guidance: Separate ephemeral data from compliance-sensitive data.
Listen for asynchronous TTL deletion and archive query needs.
commonMistakes: Using TTL for all retention problems.
Treating TTL deletion as exact scheduling.

vi.title: TTL hay archive cho retention
vi.displayQuestion: Bạn cần giữ session documents trong 30 ngày và audit events trong 7 năm. Bạn sẽ dùng TTL index, archive job hay thiết kế khác?
vi.displayIntent: Đánh giá thiết kế retention và trade-off TTL trong MongoDB.
guidance: Tách dữ liệu ephemeral khỏi dữ liệu nhạy với compliance.
Lắng nghe việc TTL delete bất đồng bộ và nhu cầu query archive.
commonMistakes: Dùng TTL cho mọi bài toán retention.
Xem TTL deletion như lịch chạy chính xác tuyệt đối.

ja.title: retention における TTL と archive
ja.displayQuestion: session documents は 30 日、audit events は 7 年保持する必要があります。TTL index、archive job、または別 design のどれを使いますか。
ja.displayIntent: MongoDB retention design と TTL trade-off を評価する。
guidance: ephemeral data と compliance-sensitive data を分ける。
TTL deletion が非同期で archive query が必要な点を確認する。
commonMistakes: すべての retention 問題に TTL を使う。
TTL deletion を正確な scheduler と考える。

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: TTL Indexes
sourceReferences.note: Used for automatic expiration behavior and TTL constraints.

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: Create TTL Indexes
sourceReferences.note: Used for TTL implementation context.

intent: Verify hands-on MongoDB migration experience from CV claims, including rollout, backfill, indexes, and validation.
primaryQuestion: Your CV mentions migrating data or changing a MongoDB schema in production. What changed, how did you backfill safely, and how did you verify correctness and performance?

expectedSignals.label: Describes the old and new schema or index design clearly.

requirements.description: Describes the old document schema, index, or data layout before the migration
Describes the new schema, index, or data layout after the migration
Explains the product, performance, or correctness reason for the migration

expectedSignals.label: Uses a safe rollout plan such as dual reads/writes, idempotent backfill, or feature flags when appropriate.

requirements.description: States the candidate's personal role or decision in the rollout or implementation
Uses dual reads/writes, feature flags, compatibility windows, or staged deploys where appropriate
Designs the backfill to be resumable and idempotent so it can recover from failures

expectedSignals.label: Mentions validation queries, sampling, counts, or checksums.

requirements.description: Uses validation queries to compare old and new data or detect missing migrated documents
Uses counts, samples, checksums, or spot checks to verify correctness
Tracks mismatch counts, error rates, or failed records during and after backfill

expectedSignals.label: Measures performance impact before and after the migration.

requirements.description: Measures query latency, write latency, or endpoint latency before and after the migration
Measures index use, documents examined, CPU, memory, or storage impact where relevant
States the metric that proved the new schema or index was better

scoringHints.description: Gives a concrete migration story with ownership, rollout safety, idempotency, validation, and measured outcome.
Real migration involvement but impact measurement or rollback is incomplete.
Likely participated but cannot explain the mechanics or evidence.
CV claim cannot be validated.

followUps.question: Which part of the migration decision or implementation was personally yours?
What metric proved the new schema or index was better?
What was the exact old shape and new shape of the document or index you migrated?
followUps.purpose: Force concrete migration context before evaluating ownership and impact.

en.title: Verify MongoDB migration ownership
en.displayQuestion: Your CV mentions migrating data or changing a MongoDB schema in production. What changed, how did you backfill safely, and how did you verify correctness and performance?
en.displayIntent: Verify practical MongoDB production migration ownership.
guidance: Ask for personal decisions and implementation details.
Expect rollout, validation, and measurable impact.
commonMistakes: Using only 'we' language.
Skipping rollback, idempotency, or validation.

vi.title: Xác minh ownership migration MongoDB
vi.displayQuestion: CV của bạn có nhắc tới migration dữ liệu hoặc đổi schema MongoDB trong production. Cụ thể thay đổi gì, bạn backfill an toàn thế nào, và verify correctness/performance ra sao?
vi.displayIntent: Xác minh ownership migration MongoDB thực tế trong production.
guidance: Hỏi rõ quyết định và phần implement cá nhân.
Kỳ vọng rollout, validation và impact đo được.
commonMistakes: Chỉ nói theo kiểu 'team/chúng tôi'.
Bỏ qua rollback, idempotency hoặc validation.

ja.title: MongoDB migration ownership の確認
ja.displayQuestion: CV に production での data migration や MongoDB schema change とあります。何を変え、どう安全に backfill し、correctness と performance をどう検証しましたか。
ja.displayIntent: 実際の MongoDB production migration ownership を確認する。
guidance: 個人の判断と実装詳細を聞く。
rollout、validation、measurable impact を期待する。
commonMistakes: 「we」だけで話す。
rollback、idempotency、validation を省略する。

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: Schema Validation
sourceReferences.note: Used for schema correctness and validation discussion.

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: Index Builds on Populated Collections
sourceReferences.note: Used for production index migration context.

intent: Evaluate senior-level judgment for sharding strategy, shard key selection, and operational trade-offs.
primaryQuestion: You need to shard a multi-tenant MongoDB workload. How would you choose a shard key, and what failure modes would you watch for after rollout?

expectedSignals.label: Evaluates query isolation, cardinality, frequency, and monotonic insert patterns.

requirements.description: Evaluates whether the shard key supports targeted query routing for common tenant queries
Evaluates cardinality and frequency of shard key values to avoid poor distribution
Evaluates whether monotonic insert patterns could create a hot shard

expectedSignals.label: Considers tenantId-based keys, compound shard keys, hashed keys, and zone/range needs.

requirements.description: Considers tenantId-based shard keys and the risk of large or uneven tenants
Considers compound or hashed shard keys to balance routing and distribution
Considers zone sharding, range placement, or data residency needs when applicable

expectedSignals.label: Watches for jumbo chunks, hot shards, scatter-gather queries, and uneven storage or write distribution.

requirements.description: Monitors uneven writes, CPU, IOPS, or operation rates across shards
Monitors jumbo chunks, chunk distribution, and balancer behavior
Monitors scatter-gather queries or queries not routed by shard key

expectedSignals.label: Plans rollout validation, metrics, and operational remediation.

requirements.description: Validates shard key choice with sample workload, cardinality analysis, or staged rollout
Defines success metrics such as balanced writes, targeted queries, and stable latency
Plans remediation such as query changes, zone changes, resharding, or data model changes if rollout exposes skew

scoringHints.description: Balances routing, distribution, write patterns, operational constraints, and post-rollout monitoring.
Good shard key reasoning but misses one failure mode or rollout concern.
Knows sharding distributes data but cannot choose a key defensibly.
No reliable sharding strategy.

followUps.question: When would tenantId alone be a bad shard key?
What cluster metrics would show that the shard key is creating hot shards?

en.title: Choose a MongoDB shard key
en.displayQuestion: You need to shard a multi-tenant MongoDB workload. How would you choose a shard key, and what failure modes would you watch for after rollout?
en.displayIntent: Evaluate senior sharding strategy and operational judgment.
guidance: Ask for query routing and data distribution assumptions.
Expect post-rollout metrics and remediation options.
commonMistakes: Choosing only the most unique field.
Ignoring hot shards and scatter-gather queries.

vi.title: Chọn shard key MongoDB
vi.displayQuestion: Bạn cần shard một workload MongoDB multi-tenant. Bạn sẽ chọn shard key như thế nào, và sau rollout bạn theo dõi failure mode nào?
vi.displayIntent: Đánh giá chiến lược sharding và judgment vận hành ở mức senior.
guidance: Hỏi giả định về query routing và data distribution.
Kỳ vọng metric sau rollout và phương án xử lý.
commonMistakes: Chỉ chọn field unique nhất.
Bỏ qua hot shard và scatter-gather query.

ja.title: MongoDB shard key の選択
ja.displayQuestion: multi-tenant MongoDB workload を shard する必要があります。shard key をどう選び、rollout 後にどんな failure mode を監視しますか。
ja.displayIntent: senior sharding strategy と operational judgment を評価する。
guidance: query routing と data distribution の仮定を聞く。
post-rollout metrics と remediation を期待する。
commonMistakes: 最も unique な field だけで選ぶ。
hot shards と scatter-gather queries を無視する。

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: Choose a Shard Key
sourceReferences.note: Used for shard key cardinality, frequency, and monotonicity considerations.

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: Sharding
sourceReferences.note: Used for sharded cluster concepts and operational failure modes.

intent: Assess senior understanding of replica sets, elections, write durability, read routing, and application-level consistency expectations.
primaryQuestion: How do MongoDB replica sets handle primary elections, write durability, and read-your-writes expectations in an application?

expectedSignals.label: Explains primary/secondary replication and election behavior at a practical level.

requirements.description: Explains that normal writes go to the primary and secondaries replicate from the oplog
Explains that a primary election can create a temporary write interruption or retryable failure
Connects replica set failover to application behavior such as reconnecting, retrying, or surfacing a transient error

expectedSignals.label: Connects write concern majority to durability across voting members.

requirements.description: Defines majority write concern as acknowledgement after the write is durable on a majority of voting data-bearing members
Connects majority acknowledgement to lower rollback risk during failover
States the latency or availability cost of waiting for majority acknowledgement compared with weaker write concern

expectedSignals.label: Explains how read preference and read concern affect stale reads and consistency.

requirements.description: Distinguishes reading from primary versus secondaries and the stale-read risk of secondary reads
Mentions read concern or majority reads when discussing stronger consistency expectations
Connects read routing and concern choices to whether the application can expect read-your-writes behavior

expectedSignals.label: Mentions retryable writes, failover handling, and application timeouts.

requirements.description: Mentions retryable writes or retry-safe driver behavior for transient failover errors
Mentions bounding waits with timeouts or server selection settings during failover
Considers idempotency or duplicate handling when the application retries writes after uncertain outcomes

scoringHints.description: Connects replica set mechanics to concrete application guarantees, failover handling, and consistency choices.
Understands replica sets but misses one application-level consistency or failover detail.
Knows primary and secondary terms but cannot reason about guarantees.
No reliable replication understanding.

followUps.question: What latency and availability trade-off comes with majority write concern?
What should the application expect during a primary election?

en.title: Replica set consistency
en.displayQuestion: How do MongoDB replica sets handle primary elections, write durability, and read-your-writes expectations in an application?
en.displayIntent: Assess senior understanding of replication and application consistency behavior.
guidance: Tie replica set internals to user-visible behavior.
Expect discussion of write concern, read preference, and failover.
commonMistakes: Assuming secondary reads are always current.
Confusing replication with sharding.

vi.title: Consistency trong replica set
vi.displayQuestion: Replica set MongoDB xử lý primary election, write durability và kỳ vọng read-your-writes trong application như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Đánh giá hiểu biết senior về replication và consistency nhìn từ application.
guidance: Liên hệ cơ chế replica set với hành vi user nhìn thấy.
Kỳ vọng thảo luận về write concern, read preference và failover.
commonMistakes: Tưởng read từ secondary luôn mới nhất.
Nhầm replication với sharding.

ja.title: replica set consistency
ja.displayQuestion: MongoDB replica set は primary election、write durability、application の read-your-writes 期待をどう扱いますか。
ja.displayIntent: replication と application consistency behavior の senior 理解を評価する。
guidance: replica set internals と user-visible behavior を結びつける。
write concern、read preference、failover の説明を期待する。
commonMistakes: secondary read が常に最新だと思う。
replication と sharding を混同する。

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: Replica Set Elections
sourceReferences.note: Used for election and failover behavior.

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: Replica Set Data Synchronization
sourceReferences.note: Used for primary-secondary replication behavior.

intent: Evaluate senior operational debugging of hot shards, uneven load, and scatter-gather behavior in a sharded MongoDB cluster.
primaryQuestion: After sharding, one shard receives most writes and some queries scatter across all shards. How would you diagnose and fix the issue?

expectedSignals.label: Checks shard key distribution, chunk distribution, balancer state, and jumbo chunks.

requirements.description: Checks whether shard key values are skewed, monotonically increasing, or dominated by a few tenants
Checks chunk distribution and whether one shard owns a disproportionate amount of active data
Checks balancer state, migration failures, and jumbo chunks that prevent balancing

expectedSignals.label: Compares query filters to shard key prefixes to identify scatter-gather patterns.

requirements.description: Reviews the slow or high-volume query shapes that scatter across shards
Compares query filters against the shard key or compound shard key prefix used for targeted routing
Identifies whether scatter-gather comes from missing shard key predicates, unsuitable indexes, or workload shape changes

expectedSignals.label: Looks at per-shard CPU, IOPS, locks, queueing, and operation metrics.

requirements.description: Compares CPU, disk IOPS, and memory pressure across shards
Compares locks, operation queues, and active operations across shards
Correlates per-shard resource imbalance with write rate, query rate, and latency symptoms

expectedSignals.label: Considers resharding, compound shard key changes, query changes, zone sharding, or data model changes.

requirements.description: Considers adding shard key predicates, changing query shapes, or adding supporting indexes before disruptive fixes
Considers resharding or compound shard key changes with their operational cost, risk, and rollout time
Considers zone sharding, tenant isolation, or data model changes when workload distribution requires them

scoringHints.description: Uses cluster metrics and query routing evidence to identify root cause and proposes realistic remediation options.
Good hot shard diagnosis but remediation trade-offs are incomplete.
Knows uneven load is bad but cannot diagnose it systematically.
No practical sharded cluster debugging approach.

followUps.question: Which per-shard metrics would you compare first?
What is the cost and risk of resharding compared with changing query patterns?
Which query shapes are missing the shard key prefix and causing scatter-gather routing?
followUps.purpose: Check whether diagnosis uses actual routed query shapes.

en.title: Debug hot shard behavior
en.displayQuestion: After sharding, one shard receives most writes and some queries scatter across all shards. How would you diagnose and fix the issue?
en.displayIntent: Evaluate senior debugging of hot shards and scatter-gather queries.
guidance: Expect evidence from distribution, routing, and per-shard metrics.
Ask for remediation options and their risks.
commonMistakes: Adding shards without fixing shard key or query routing.
Ignoring whether queries include shard key fields.

vi.title: Debug hot shard
vi.displayQuestion: Sau khi sharding, một shard nhận phần lớn write và một số query scatter qua tất cả shard. Bạn diagnose và fix vấn đề này như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Đánh giá debug hot shard và scatter-gather query ở mức senior.
guidance: Kỳ vọng bằng chứng từ distribution, routing và metric từng shard.
Hỏi các phương án xử lý và rủi ro tương ứng.
commonMistakes: Thêm shard mà không sửa shard key hoặc query routing.
Bỏ qua việc query có field shard key hay không.

ja.title: hot shard behavior の debug
ja.displayQuestion: sharding 後、1 shard に write が集中し、一部 query が全 shard に scatter しています。どう診断し修正しますか。
ja.displayIntent: hot shards と scatter-gather queries の senior debugging を評価する。
guidance: distribution、routing、per-shard metrics の証拠を期待する。
remediation options と risk を聞く。
commonMistakes: shard key や query routing を直さず shard を追加する。
query が shard key fields を含むか無視する。

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: Sharded Cluster Query Routing
sourceReferences.note: Used for targeted queries versus scatter-gather behavior.

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: Sharded Cluster Balancer
sourceReferences.note: Used for balancing and chunk distribution context.

intent: Evaluate senior design trade-offs between MongoDB multi-document transactions, document modeling, idempotency, and event-driven consistency.
primaryQuestion: For a checkout flow that updates inventory, creates an order, records payment state, and publishes events, where would you use MongoDB transactions, and where would you prefer idempotency or eventual consistency?

expectedSignals.label: Identifies strong invariants that may require a transaction, such as avoiding oversell for a specific stock record.

requirements.description: Names the concrete checkout invariants that must be protected, such as inventory not going below zero for a stock record
Scopes the transaction to the MongoDB writes that must commit or abort together
Mentions conditional inventory update, unique constraints, or equivalent checks to prevent oversell

expectedSignals.label: Separates external payment calls from database transaction boundaries.

requirements.description: Keeps external payment API calls outside the MongoDB transaction boundary
Explains that holding a database transaction while waiting on an external service increases lock time, abort risk, and uncertainty
Records payment intent, authorization, or reconciliation state in MongoDB without assuming the external call is atomic with the database commit

expectedSignals.label: Uses idempotency keys and outbox/event patterns for reliable event publication.

requirements.description: Uses an idempotency key or unique request identifier to prevent duplicate order creation on retries
Uses an outbox or durable event record written with the order state before asynchronous publication
Handles event publication retry and deduplication instead of assuming the transaction publishes events reliably

expectedSignals.label: Considers latency, lock duration, retries, and failure compensation.

requirements.description: Limits transaction duration and document scope to reduce latency, lock pressure, and abort probability
Plans retries for transient transaction errors and unknown commit results
Plans compensation or reconciliation for failures after payment, event publication, or inventory reservation steps

scoringHints.description: Draws clear consistency boundaries, uses transactions narrowly, and handles external side effects with idempotency/outbox thinking.
Good transaction judgment but event publication or payment failure handling is incomplete.
Uses transactions broadly without understanding side effects and retries.
Cannot reason about checkout consistency.

followUps.question: What should not be inside the MongoDB transaction, and why?
How would you prevent duplicate order creation if the client retries?

en.title: Checkout transaction boundaries
en.displayQuestion: For a checkout flow that updates inventory, creates an order, records payment state, and publishes events, where would you use MongoDB transactions, and where would you prefer idempotency or eventual consistency?
en.displayIntent: Evaluate senior consistency boundaries in a MongoDB-backed checkout design.
guidance: Ask the candidate to separate database writes from external side effects.
Expect idempotency and event reliability discussion.
commonMistakes: Putting external payment calls inside a database transaction.
Ignoring duplicate requests and retry behavior.

vi.title: Boundary transaction cho checkout
vi.displayQuestion: Với checkout flow cần update inventory, tạo order, ghi payment state và publish events, phần nào bạn dùng MongoDB transaction, phần nào dùng idempotency hoặc eventual consistency?
vi.displayIntent: Đánh giá boundary consistency ở mức senior trong thiết kế checkout dùng MongoDB.
guidance: Yêu cầu ứng viên tách database write khỏi external side effect.
Kỳ vọng thảo luận về idempotency và độ tin cậy event.
commonMistakes: Đưa external payment call vào database transaction.
Bỏ qua duplicate request và retry behavior.

ja.title: checkout transaction boundary
ja.displayQuestion: inventory update、order creation、payment state 記録、event publish を含む checkout flow で、どこに MongoDB transaction を使い、どこに idempotency や eventual consistency を使いますか。
ja.displayIntent: MongoDB-backed checkout design の senior consistency boundary を評価する。
guidance: database writes と external side effects を分ける。
idempotency と event reliability の説明を期待する。
commonMistakes: external payment call を database transaction に入れる。
duplicate requests と retry behavior を無視する。

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: Transactions Production Considerations
sourceReferences.note: Used for transaction operational trade-offs.

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: Retryable Writes
sourceReferences.note: Used for retry and idempotency discussion.

intent: Assess senior approach to schema evolution, validation, backward compatibility, and mixed-version application deployments.
primaryQuestion: How would you evolve a MongoDB document schema safely when old application versions may still read and write documents during a rolling deployment?

expectedSignals.label: Designs backward-compatible reads and writes for mixed document versions.

requirements.description: Designs code that can read both old and new document shapes during the rolling deployment
Accounts for old application versions that may still write the old shape while new code is deployed
Keeps rollback safe by avoiding a data shape that old code cannot tolerate

expectedSignals.label: Uses additive changes, default handling, and version fields or feature flags when useful.

requirements.description: Prefers additive fields or tolerant readers before removing or renaming existing fields
Handles missing new fields with defaults or derived values instead of assuming every document is already migrated
Uses schema version fields or feature flags when the application must distinguish old and new behavior

expectedSignals.label: Plans idempotent backfill and validation after readers are compatible.

requirements.description: Plans a resumable and idempotent backfill that can be rerun safely
Runs backfill only after deployed readers can handle both shapes
Defines validation queries, counts, samples, or checksums to prove affected documents were migrated correctly

expectedSignals.label: Introduces schema validation carefully after data and application compatibility are proven.

requirements.description: Delays strict schema validation until old writers are gone and existing data is compatible
Considers warn mode, partial validation, or staged enforcement before blocking writes
Monitors validation failures and rollout health before increasing enforcement

scoringHints.description: Explains a staged compatibility plan covering readers, writers, backfill, validation, rollback, and monitoring.
Good migration approach but misses validation timing or rollback.
Understands schema changes but not rolling deployment hazards.
No safe schema evolution plan.

followUps.question: How would old code behave when it sees a document already written in the new shape?
What validation would prove the backfill covered all affected documents?
What can go wrong if strict schema validation is enabled before all application versions are compatible?

en.title: Safe MongoDB schema evolution
en.displayQuestion: How would you evolve a MongoDB document schema safely when old application versions may still read and write documents during a rolling deployment?
en.displayIntent: Evaluate senior schema evolution and compatibility planning.
guidance: Ask for a staged rollout sequence.
Expect compatibility, backfill, validation, and rollback discussion.
commonMistakes: Assuming all data is instantly in the new shape.
Adding strict validation before old writers are gone.

vi.title: Evolve schema MongoDB an toàn
vi.displayQuestion: Bạn evolve schema document MongoDB an toàn như thế nào khi các version application cũ vẫn có thể read/write trong rolling deployment?
vi.displayIntent: Đánh giá kế hoạch schema evolution và compatibility ở mức senior.
guidance: Yêu cầu trình tự rollout theo giai đoạn.
Kỳ vọng thảo luận về compatibility, backfill, validation và rollback.
commonMistakes: Tưởng toàn bộ dữ liệu lập tức có shape mới.
Thêm strict validation trước khi writer cũ biến mất.

ja.title: 安全な MongoDB schema evolution
ja.displayQuestion: rolling deployment 中に古い application version が document を read/write する可能性がある場合、MongoDB document schema をどう安全に evolve しますか。
ja.displayIntent: senior schema evolution と compatibility planning を評価する。
guidance: staged rollout sequence を聞く。
compatibility、backfill、validation、rollback の説明を期待する。
commonMistakes: すべての data が即座に新しい shape になると考える。
古い writer が残る前に strict validation を追加する。

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: Schema Validation
sourceReferences.note: Used for validation timing and schema rules.

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: Data Modeling Patterns
sourceReferences.note: Used for schema evolution and modeling pattern context.

intent: Verify senior production ownership through a concrete MongoDB performance or reliability incident.
primaryQuestion: Tell me about a serious MongoDB performance or reliability incident you owned. What was the symptom, root cause, mitigation, permanent fix, and measured outcome?

expectedSignals.label: Frames the incident with user/business impact and timeline.

requirements.description: States the user-visible or business impact of the MongoDB incident
Gives a concrete timeline from symptom detection through mitigation and recovery
Identifies the part of the incident response or system area the candidate personally owned

expectedSignals.label: Uses evidence from metrics, logs, query plans, replication/sharding state, or deployment changes.

requirements.description: Uses MongoDB-specific metrics such as latency, CPU, IOPS, locks, replication lag, cache pressure, or operation rate
Uses query plans, slow logs, index usage, shard distribution, or replica state to support the root cause
Correlates the incident with a traffic pattern, deployment, data growth, index change, or topology event

expectedSignals.label: Distinguishes immediate mitigation from durable fix.

requirements.description: States the immediate mitigation used to reduce user impact, such as rollback, throttling, index change, query disablement, failover, or capacity action
States the durable fix that removed or reduced the root cause rather than only treating symptoms
Explains the trade-off or risk accepted during the temporary mitigation

expectedSignals.label: Provides measured outcome and prevention such as alerts, tests, runbooks, or capacity planning.

requirements.description: Provides a before/after metric for the permanent fix, such as p95 latency, error rate, lag, CPU, or throughput
Adds prevention such as alerts, load tests, query review, runbooks, or capacity planning
Connects the prevention step directly to the incident root cause instead of listing generic process improvements

scoringHints.description: Shows senior incident ownership with clear evidence, decision-making, communication, durable prevention, and measurable impact.
Real incident story but metrics, communication, or prevention detail is incomplete.
Participated in an incident but ownership and technical diagnosis are unclear.
Incident claim cannot be verified.

followUps.question: What metric moved after the permanent fix, and what was the before/after value?
What decision did you personally make during the incident?
What was the incident timeline, blast radius, and user or business impact?
What did you do immediately to reduce impact, and how was that different from the permanent fix?
followUps.purpose: Separate temporary mitigation from durable remediation.

en.title: Verify MongoDB incident ownership
en.displayQuestion: Tell me about a serious MongoDB performance or reliability incident you owned. What was the symptom, root cause, mitigation, permanent fix, and measured outcome?
en.displayIntent: Verify senior MongoDB production ownership through an incident story.
guidance: Ask for timeline, evidence, and communication.
Separate mitigation from permanent prevention.
commonMistakes: Giving a generic outage story with no MongoDB-specific signal.
Skipping measured outcome or prevention.

vi.title: Xác minh ownership incident MongoDB
vi.displayQuestion: Hãy kể về một incident nghiêm trọng về performance hoặc reliability MongoDB mà bạn owned. Symptom, root cause, mitigation, permanent fix và outcome đo được là gì?
vi.displayIntent: Xác minh ownership production MongoDB ở mức senior qua câu chuyện incident.
guidance: Hỏi timeline, bằng chứng và communication.
Tách mitigation trước mắt khỏi prevention lâu dài.
commonMistakes: Kể outage chung chung không có tín hiệu cụ thể của MongoDB.
Bỏ qua outcome đo được hoặc prevention.

ja.title: MongoDB incident ownership の確認
ja.displayQuestion: あなたが owner だった重大な MongoDB performance または reliability incident について話してください。symptom、root cause、mitigation、permanent fix、measured outcome は何でしたか。
ja.displayIntent: incident story を通じて senior MongoDB production ownership を確認する。
guidance: timeline、evidence、communication を聞く。
mitigation と permanent prevention を分ける。
commonMistakes: MongoDB-specific signal のない一般的な outage story を話す。
measured outcome や prevention を省略する。

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: Monitoring for MongoDB
sourceReferences.note: Used for operational metrics and incident investigation context.

sourceReferences.label: MongoDB Docs: Analyze Query Performance
sourceReferences.note: Used for query-plan evidence during performance incidents.
